**Patient Demographics:**
Elvira Valadez, a 55-year-old female. Race: Non-hispanic black. Language: English.

**Problems:**
Hypertension. Hyperlipidemia. Obstructive sleep apnea. Congestive heart failure (ejection fraction 35-40%). Ischemic Stroke (07/02/2024). Anaphylaxis (11/01/2025), resolved. Coronary Artery Disease, Two-Vessel (03/22/2026).

**Hospitalizations & Urgent Events:**

Not documented

Attending Name: Not Documented
Discharge Disposition: Home with responsible adult
Discharge Summary Present (Yes/No): No

Hospital Course Summary:
Elvira Valadez-Nucleus, a 32-year-old female, presented to the ED via EMS with acute anaphylaxis after ingesting shrimp, experiencing difficulty breathing, diffuse urticaria, and facial angioedema. She received two doses of IM Epinephrine, IV Diphenhydramine, IV Famotidine, and oxygen. A chest x-ray was negative, and serum tryptase was elevated at 18.5 mcg/L, confirming anaphylaxis. After a 3-hour observation period, her symptoms resolved, and vital signs stabilized. She was educated on shellfish avoidance, EpiPen use, and the risk of biphasic reactions. Discharge was to home with a responsible adult, with urgent follow-up recommended with an allergist and PCP.

Admit Diagnosis(es) or Reasons for admission:
Acute anaphylaxis due to shellfish ingestion with difficulty breathing and diffuse urticaria.

documentReferences.document_reference_content_data:
CARDIOLOGY VISIT NOTE

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
Age/Sex: 55F
Date of Visit: 04/08/2025
Provider: Dr. Sarah Chen
Visit Type: Routine Follow-up

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Patient presents for routine cardiology follow-up. Recent pharmacologic stress test with nuclear imaging performed on 03/20/2025 was reviewed in detail. Patient reports stable symptomatology with mild exertional dyspnea noted only with heavy activity. Denies chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema. Blood pressure remains well-controlled on current antihypertensive therapy. Overall, patient tolerating current management well.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION & REVIEW OF DIAGNOSTIC STUDIES:
Vital signs: BP 132/82 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, SpO2 97% on room air. Stress test results demonstrate LVEF 42% with small fixed inferior defect consistent with prior myocardial infarction. No reversible ischemia or inducible ischemic changes noted. Current medications include Lisinopril for blood pressure and heart failure management.

ASSESSMENT & PLAN:
1. Chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF ~42%) – Stable on current regimen
2. Hypertension – Well-controlled on current therapy
Continue current medications. Routine laboratory studies ordered. Transthoracic echocardiogram scheduled in 4 months for reassessment of ventricular function. Patient to follow up in clinic in 4 months or sooner if symptoms develop. Patient counseled regarding activity limitation and given clear return precautions.

SLEEP MEDICINE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
Age/Sex: 55F
Date of Visit: 10/15/2024
Provider: Dr. Michael Torres

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Valadez-Nucleus returns to clinic for 6-month follow-up evaluation of obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) management on continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy. Patient reports significant improvement in daytime symptoms since initiating CPAP treatment. She notes substantial reduction in daytime sleepiness, improved overall sleep quality, and increased energy levels throughout the day. Vitals today: BP 134/84, HR 74 bpm, SpO2 96% on room air.

OBJECTIVE DATA & ASSESSMENT:
Review of CPAP device data reveals excellent compliance with average nightly usage of 6.2 hours. Residual apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) is 3.1 events per hour, which is well below therapeutic target. The 90th percentile pressure requirement is 12.4 cmH2O with normal mask leak rates. Epworth Sleepiness Scale has improved significantly from 14 to 6, indicating marked subjective symptom improvement. Assessment: Obstructive sleep apnea well-controlled on current CPAP therapy with excellent adherence and symptom resolution.

PLAN:
Patient to continue current CPAP settings at present pressure. Emphasized importance of nightly CPAP use for sustained therapeutic benefit. Patient demonstrated good understanding and commitment to treatment regimen. Routine follow-up appointment scheduled for one year or sooner if any concerns arise.

CARDIOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE

PATIENT NAME: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
DATE OF BIRTH: <UNKNOWN - Age 55 years>
SEX: Female
DATE OF CONSULTATION: February 10, 2026
LOCATION: Austin Memorial Hospital
CONSULTING PHYSICIAN: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD
ADMITTING PHYSICIAN: <UNKNOWN>

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

REASON FOR CONSULT:
Management of acute decompensated heart failure (ADHF) with CHF exacerbation. Patient admitted 02/09/2026.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
The patient is a 55-year-old female admitted to Austin Memorial Hospital on February 9, 2026, for acute decompensated heart failure exacerbation. She presents with clinical evidence of volume overload and pulmonary congestion. Recent transthoracic echocardiography obtained on January 10, 2026, demonstrated significant systolic dysfunction with LVEF 35-40%, moderate left ventricular dilation, moderate mitral regurgitation (MR), and moderate left atrial (LA) enlargement.

Current laboratory studies reveal:
• NT-proBNP: 132 pg/mL
• High-sensitivity Troponin: Trending 2-6 ng/L (below myocardial infarction cutoff)
• C-reactive protein (CRP): 12.4 mg/dL

Chest X-ray findings show bilateral pleural effusions consistent with pulmonary edema.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
• Hypertension
• Hyperlipidemia
• Prior ischemic stroke
• Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA) - currently on CPAP therapy

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
• Aspirin 81 mg daily
• Atorvastatin 40 mg daily
• Clopidogrel 75 mg daily
• Lisinopril 10 mg daily
• NOTE: No current diuretic therapy on admission

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ASSESSMENT:

1. ACUTE DECOMPENSATED HEART FAILURE (ADHF)
- Systolic dysfunction with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF): LVEF 35-40%
- Pulmonary and peripheral volume overload with bilateral pleural effusions
- Secondary mitral regurgitation contributing to hemodynamic compromise
- Left atrial enlargement likely secondary to chronic mitral insufficiency

2. ETIOLOGY CONSIDERATIONS:
- Prior ischemic stroke history warrants consideration of ischemic cardiomyopathy
- Current troponin elevation (2-6 ng/L) is modest and trending; not diagnostic of acute MI
- Elevated NT-proBNP (132 pg/mL) consistent with decompensated state
- Mild inflammatory marker elevation (CRP 12.4)

3. MEDICATION OPTIMIZATION GAPS:
- No diuretic agent currently prescribed; contributing to volume overload
- ACE inhibitor dosed at 10 mg (potentially subtherapeutic)
- Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) appropriate given prior stroke history

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

RECOMMENDATIONS:

ACUTE MANAGEMENT:
1. Initiate intravenous diuresis targeting net negative 1.0-1.5 L/day to achieve euvolemic state
- Monitor urine output, daily weights, and vital signs closely
- Adjust IV diuretic dosing based on clinical response and renal function

2. Continue current ACE inhibitor therapy (Lisinopril 10 mg daily)
- Consider uptitration once acute decompensation resolves and renal function stabilized

TRANSITION TO OUTPATIENT REGIMEN:
3. Transition to oral Furosemide 20 mg daily when patient achieves euvolemic state
- Reassess diuretic requirement during outpatient follow-up

SUPPORTIVE CARE:
4. Implement strict 2 L/day fluid restriction
5. Implement low-sodium diet (<2 g/day sodium)
6. Daily weight monitoring; report gains >2-3 lbs to primary care provider

MONITORING:
7. Repeat echocardiography in 3-6 months to reassess LVEF and degree of mitral regurgitation
8. Monitor renal function and electrolytes closely during diuretic initiation and titration
9. Continue CPAP compliance monitoring for OSA management

FOLLOW-UP:
10. Outpatient cardiology follow-up appointment to be scheduled 4 weeks post-discharge
- Assess tolerance of oral diuretic regimen
- Evaluate for need for additional heart failure medications (beta-blocker, aldosterone antagonist)
- Consider device therapy evaluation (ICD/CRT) based on LVEF persistently <35% and NYHA class

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CLINICAL IMPRESSION:
Ms. Valadez-Nucleus presents with acute decompensated heart failure secondary to systolic dysfunction with reduced ejection fraction. Her current presentation is consistent with acute volume overload with pulmonary and pleural involvement. Urgent diuresis with transition to oral regimen and strict dietary/fluid management should facilitate clinical improvement. Comprehensive outpatient cardiology evaluation will be necessary to optimize her chronic heart failure regimen and assess candidacy for advanced therapies.

We appreciate the opportunity to participate in her care.

Respectfully submitted,

_________________________________

Cardiology Consultant
Austin Memorial Hospital

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus

Date of Visit: 12/02/2025
Provider: Dr. Sarah Chen
Visit Type: Routine Follow-up

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Patient presents for routine cardiology follow-up. She reports overall stable cardiac symptoms but notes a mild increase in exertional dyspnea over the past 2-3 weeks, which she attributes to increased sodium intake during the holiday season. Denies chest pain, orthopnea, or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. Patient reports good compliance with CPAP therapy. Weight has increased 4 lbs since last visit (current weight 173 lbs). On examination, mild bilateral ankle edema is noted.

VITAL SIGNS AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Blood pressure 136/84 mmHg, heart rate 76 bpm (regular), SpO2 95% on room air. EKG shows normal sinus rhythm at rate 76 with stable left ventricular hypertrophy, unchanged from prior studies.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
Assessment: (1) Congestive heart failure with mild volume overload, likely secondary to dietary sodium indiscretion, currently not requiring diuretic therapy but will monitor closely; (2) Hypertension, slightly above goal on current regimen; (3) Hyperlipidemia, lipid panel ordered. Patient counseled extensively on strict sodium restriction to help resolve current volume status. Lipid panel has been ordered for 12/30/2025. Transthoracic echocardiogram scheduled for January 2026 for interval assessment. Will consider initiation of diuretic therapy if symptoms worsen. Patient instructed to return for follow-up in 4-6 weeks or sooner if experiencing worsening dyspnea, chest pain, or increased edema.

CLINICAL INDICATION/HISTORY

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus, 55-year-old female
Date of Procedure: 03/22/2026
Referring Physician: Dr. Sarah Chen

55-year-old female with ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF 35-40%), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia presenting for diagnostic left heart catheterization to evaluate coronary anatomy. Patient has significant cardiac history including prior ischemic stroke in 2022 and was hospitalized for acute CHF exacerbation from 02/09-02/12/2026. Recent transthoracic echocardiogram from January 2026 demonstrates decline in left ventricular ejection fraction to 35-40% with moderate mitral regurgitation. Nuclear stress testing performed in March 2025 revealed LVEF of 42% with a small fixed inferior defect and no evidence of reversible ischemia at that time. Given the progressive decline in cardiac function and need to define coronary anatomy, catheterization is being performed to guide further revascularization and heart failure management strategies.

PATIENT NAME: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
DATE OF BIRTH: 12/26/1970

PHYSICIAN: Sarah Chen, MD

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Follow-up after hospitalization for congestive heart failure exacerbation

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Valadez-Nucleus is a 55-year-old female presenting for a 4-week post-discharge follow-up visit following hospitalization from 02/09/2026 to 02/12/2026 for CHF exacerbation. She reports significant clinical improvement since her discharge.

Her dyspnea has markedly improved and she now tolerates climbing one flight of stairs without significant breathlessness. Lower extremity edema, which was present at the time of hospitalization, has completely resolved. She denies orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or chest pain.

The patient has demonstrated excellent medication compliance, taking all prescribed medications as directed, including Furosemide 20 mg daily. She has been adherent to a low-sodium diet and fluid restriction (intake limited to 2 liters per day). Current weight is approximately 168 lbs, which is stable since discharge and represents a significant improvement from her pre-hospitalization weight.

Regarding her sleep apnea management, she reports excellent compliance with CPAP therapy, using the device nightly. CPAP settings are maintained at 12 cmH2O via full face mask, with an average of 6 hours of use per night. Device data download confirms good compliance. She specifically denies current use of supplemental home oxygen.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

POSITIVE: Mild dyspnea on exertion with heavy exertion (baseline, improved from hospitalization)

NEGATIVE:
- Chest pain or pressure
- Palpitations
- Syncope or presyncope
- Orthopnea
- Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea
- Lower extremity edema
- Nocturia
- Fatigue (improved)
- Fever, chills
- Cough
- Shortness of breath at rest
- Headache
- Vision changes
- Neurological deficits
- Gastrointestinal symptoms

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:

1. Congestive Heart Failure (Systolic) - EF 35-40% as of 01/10/2026 echocardiogram
2. Hypertension
3. Hyperlipidemia
4. Ischemic Stroke (remote, on antithrombotic therapy)
5. Obstructive Sleep Apnea

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

MEDICATIONS:

1. Furosemide 20 mg PO daily
2. [ACE inhibitor/ARB - dosage per discharge papers]
3. [Beta-blocker - dosage per discharge papers]
4. Atorvastatin [current dose per discharge papers]
5. Antiplatelet agent(s) for stroke prevention
6. [Additional medications per discharge summary]

Patient reports 100% compliance with prescribed regimen.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

RECENT LABORATORY RESULTS:

LIPID PANEL (03/10/2026):
- Total Cholesterol: 200 mg/dL
- HDL Cholesterol: 50 mg/dL
- LDL Cholesterol: 133 mg/dL
- Triglycerides: 123 mg/dL
- VLDL: 32 mg/dL

Previous Lipid Panel (12/30/2025):
- Total Cholesterol: 250 mg/dL
- HDL Cholesterol: 78 mg/dL (note: appears elevated; verify)
- LDL Cholesterol: 150 mg/dL
- Triglycerides: 156 mg/dL

Assessment: Improvement noted in lipid panel; however, LDL remains above goal.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

VITAL SIGNS:
- Blood Pressure: 128/80 mmHg (well-controlled)
- Heart Rate: 72 bpm (regular, normal rate)
- Respiratory Rate: 16 (not documented but normal assumed)
- Temperature: [Not documented - assumed afebrile]
- Weight: 168 lbs (stable since discharge)

GENERAL: Alert, oriented, comfortable at rest. No acute distress.

CARDIOVASCULAR: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated. No jugular venous distension.

PULMONARY: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, rhonchi, or wheezes. No signs of pulmonary edema.

EXTREMITIES: Trace bilateral ankle edema (improved from hospitalization). Extremities warm with good perfusion. No cyanosis. Pedal pulses intact and symmetric bilaterally.

ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Liver edge not palpable. No ascites.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ASSESSMENT:

1. CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE, SYSTOLIC (EF 35-40%)
- Stable on current medical regimen
- Significant clinical improvement from hospitalization
- No current signs of decompensation
- Excellent medication and dietary compliance

2. HYPERTENSION
- Well-controlled on current medication regimen
- BP 128/80 mmHg today

3. HYPERLIPIDEMIA
- LDL cholesterol 133 mg/dL, above goal of <70 mg/dL for patient with CHF and prior stroke
- Triglycerides improved to 123 mg/dL (from 156 mg/dL)
- Recommend intensification of statin therapy

4. ISCHEMIC STROKE (Remote)
- Stable on current antithrombotic therapy
- No acute neurological deficits

5. OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNEA
- Well-controlled with CPAP therapy
- Excellent compliance (6 hours nightly at 12 cmH2O)
- No current indication for supplemental home oxygen
- Device data confirms adequate therapy adherence

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PLAN:

1. CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE:
- Continue current heart failure medications (diuretic, ACE-I/ARB, beta-blocker)
- Maintain low-sodium diet (<2 grams sodium daily) and fluid restriction (2 L/day)
- Continue daily weight monitoring; report weight gain >2-3 lbs in 1-2 days
- Echocardiogram to be repeated in 6 months to reassess ventricular function
- Patient educated regarding warning signs of decompensation (increased dyspnea, weight gain, orthopnea, increased edema)

2. HYPERTENSION:
- Continue current antihypertensive medications
- BP well-controlled; no medication changes at this time
- Home blood pressure monitoring recommended

3. HYPERLIPIDEMIA:
- INCREASE Atorvastatin to 80 mg PO daily (was on lower dose per discharge papers)
- Goal LDL <70 mg/dL given CAD risk factors (prior stroke, CHF)
- Repeat lipid panel in 3 months to reassess response to increased statin dose
- Continue dietary modifications (low saturated fat, low cholesterol diet)

4. ISCHEMIC STROKE PREVENTION:
- Continue current antithrombotic therapy as prescribed
- No changes at this time

5. OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNEA:
- Continue CPAP therapy with current settings (12 cmH2O, full face mask)
- Continue nightly use as tolerated
- Patient advised NO indication for supplemental home oxygen at this time
- CPAP device to be serviced per manufacturer recommendations
- Follow-up with sleep medicine if any changes in symptoms or compliance

6. FOLLOW-UP:
- Return to cardiology clinic in 3 months (approximately 06/10/2026)
- Lipid panel to be drawn prior to or at 3-month visit
- Echocardiogram to be scheduled for 6 months post-visit
- Patient to notify office of any chest pain, severe dyspnea, weight gain, new symptoms, or medication side effects
- Office phone number provided for urgent concerns

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PATIENT EDUCATION:

Ms. Valadez-Nucleus was counseled on:
- Importance of continued medication compliance
- Low-sodium diet and fluid restriction
- Daily weight monitoring and reporting significant changes
- Warning signs of CHF exacerbation
- Continued CPAP use for sleep apnea management
- Importance of lipid control in cardiac and stroke prevention
- Activity level appropriate for her current status (no strenuous activity without clearance)

Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and agreed with recommendations.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CLINICAL IMPRESSION:

This is a 55-year-old female with systolic CHF (EF 35-40%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and prior ischemic stroke who presents for post-hospitalization follow-up 4 weeks after discharge for CHF exacerbation. She has made an excellent clinical recovery with marked improvement in dyspnea and resolution of edema. She demonstrates outstanding compliance with medical therapy, dietary recommendations, and CPAP management. Her blood pressure is well-controlled. Lipid panel shows improvement but LDL remains above goal, warranting intensification of statin therapy. She will continue current medications with the addition of increased Atorvastatin dosing, and will follow up in clinic in 3 months.

2006-06-17T15:19:11-04:00 Diabetes self management plan http://snomed.info/sct 698360004

HOSPITAL DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS - CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE (CHF) EXACERBATION

MONITORING YOUR HEALTH AT HOME:
It is very important to monitor your fluid status daily. Please weigh yourself every morning before eating and use the same scale each time. Write down your weight each day. Call us immediately if your weight increases by more than 2 pounds in a single day or more than 5 pounds in a week, as this may indicate fluid buildup that needs attention. Additionally, elevate your legs when sitting down to help reduce swelling. Be sure to continue using your CPAP machine every night to treat your sleep apnea, as this helps your heart work better while you sleep.

DIET, FLUIDS, AND MEDICATIONS:
Follow a low-sodium diet with less than 2,000 mg (2 grams) of salt per day to prevent fluid retention. This means limiting canned foods, processed meats, and adding salt to your meals. Restrict your total fluid intake to 2 liters (about 8 cups) per day, including water, juice, and other beverages. Take all your medications exactly as prescribed. You have a new medication called Furosemide 20 mg that you will take once daily—this is a water pill that helps remove extra fluid from your body. Do not skip doses or adjust your medications without talking to your doctor first.

WHEN TO SEEK IMMEDIATE HELP:
Call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room right away if you experience worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, severe dizziness, or a sudden increase in swelling in your legs or abdomen. These could be signs of a serious problem that needs urgent care. Schedule a follow-up appointment with your cardiologist, Dr. Sarah Chen, for 4 weeks from now (March 10, 2026). If you have any questions about these instructions, please call our heart failure clinic before you leave the hospital today.
<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">There is no current social history at the time of this document's creation.</text>

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3"><paragraph>Follow up with cardiologist Dr. Sarah Chen in 4 weeks (03/10/2026). Continue all discharge medications. Daily weight monitoring. Low-sodium diet (<2000mg/day). Fluid restriction (2L/day). Continue CPAP therapy nightly. Call for weight gain >2 lbs/day or >5 lbs/week, worsening SOB, chest pain, or increased swelling.</paragraph></text>

CARDIOLOGY OFFICE VISIT NOTE

PATIENT NAME: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
DATE OF BIRTH: 12/26/1970
AGE: 55
SEX: Female
DATE OF VISIT: 03/10/2026
PHYSICIAN: Sarah Chen, MD
LOCATION: Austin Heart & Lung Specialists

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Follow-up after hospitalization for congestive heart failure exacerbation

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Valadez-Nucleus is a 55-year-old female presenting for a 4-week post-discharge follow-up visit following hospitalization from 02/09/2026 to 02/12/2026 for CHF exacerbation. She reports significant clinical improvement since her discharge.

Her dyspnea has markedly improved and she now tolerates climbing one flight of stairs without significant breathlessness. Lower extremity edema, which was present at the time of hospitalization, has completely resolved. She denies orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or chest pain.

The patient has demonstrated excellent medication compliance, taking all prescribed medications as directed, including Furosemide 20 mg daily. She has been adherent to a low-sodium diet and fluid restriction (intake limited to 2 liters per day). Current weight is approximately 168 lbs, which is stable since discharge and represents a significant improvement from her pre-hospitalization weight.

Regarding her sleep apnea management, she reports excellent compliance with CPAP therapy, using the device nightly. CPAP settings are maintained at 12 cmH2O via full face mask, with an average of 6 hours of use per night. Device data download confirms good compliance. She specifically denies current use of supplemental home oxygen.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

POSITIVE: Mild dyspnea on exertion with heavy exertion (baseline, improved from hospitalization)

NEGATIVE:
- Chest pain or pressure
- Palpitations
- Syncope or presyncope
- Orthopnea
- Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea
- Lower extremity edema
- Nocturia
- Fatigue (improved)
- Fever, chills
- Cough
- Shortness of breath at rest
- Headache
- Vision changes
- Neurological deficits
- Gastrointestinal symptoms

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:

1. Congestive Heart Failure (Systolic) - EF 35-40% as of 01/10/2026 echocardiogram
2. Hypertension
3. Hyperlipidemia
4. Ischemic Stroke (remote, on antithrombotic therapy)
5. Obstructive Sleep Apnea

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

MEDICATIONS:

1. Furosemide 20 mg PO daily
2. [ACE inhibitor/ARB - dosage per discharge papers]
3. [Beta-blocker - dosage per discharge papers]
4. Atorvastatin [current dose per discharge papers]
5. Antiplatelet agent(s) for stroke prevention
6. [Additional medications per discharge summary]

Patient reports 100% compliance with prescribed regimen.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

RECENT LABORATORY RESULTS:

LIPID PANEL (03/10/2026):
- Total Cholesterol: 200 mg/dL
- HDL Cholesterol: 50 mg/dL
- LDL Cholesterol: 133 mg/dL
- Triglycerides: 123 mg/dL
- VLDL: 32 mg/dL

Previous Lipid Panel (12/30/2025):
- Total Cholesterol: 250 mg/dL
- HDL Cholesterol: 78 mg/dL (note: appears elevated; verify)
- LDL Cholesterol: 150 mg/dL
- Triglycerides: 156 mg/dL

Assessment: Improvement noted in lipid panel; however, LDL remains above goal.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

VITAL SIGNS:
- Blood Pressure: 128/80 mmHg (well-controlled)
- Heart Rate: 72 bpm (regular, normal rate)
- Respiratory Rate: 16 (not documented but normal assumed)
- Temperature: [Not documented - assumed afebrile]
- Weight: 168 lbs (stable since discharge)

GENERAL: Alert, oriented, comfortable at rest. No acute distress.

CARDIOVASCULAR: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated. No jugular venous distension.

PULMONARY: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, rhonchi, or wheezes. No signs of pulmonary edema.

EXTREMITIES: Trace bilateral ankle edema (improved from hospitalization). Extremities warm with good perfusion. No cyanosis. Pedal pulses intact and symmetric bilaterally.

ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Liver edge not palpable. No ascites.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ASSESSMENT:

1. CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE, SYSTOLIC (EF 35-40%)
- Stable on current medical regimen
- Significant clinical improvement from hospitalization
- No current signs of decompensation
- Excellent medication and dietary compliance

2. HYPERTENSION
- Well-controlled on current medication regimen
- BP 128/80 mmHg today

3. HYPERLIPIDEMIA
- LDL cholesterol 133 mg/dL, above goal of <70 mg/dL for patient with CHF and prior stroke
- Triglycerides improved to 123 mg/dL (from 156 mg/dL)
- Recommend intensification of statin therapy

4. ISCHEMIC STROKE (Remote)
- Stable on current antithrombotic therapy
- No acute neurological deficits

5. OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNEA
- Well-controlled with CPAP therapy
- Excellent compliance (6 hours nightly at 12 cmH2O)
- No current indication for supplemental home oxygen
- Device data confirms adequate therapy adherence

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PLAN:

1. CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE:
- Continue current heart failure medications (diuretic, ACE-I/ARB, beta-blocker)
- Maintain low-sodium diet (<2 grams sodium daily) and fluid restriction (2 L/day)
- Continue daily weight monitoring; report weight gain >2-3 lbs in 1-2 days
- Echocardiogram to be repeated in 6 months to reassess ventricular function
- Patient educated regarding warning signs of decompensation (increased dyspnea, weight gain, orthopnea, increased edema)

2. HYPERTENSION:
- Continue current antihypertensive medications
- BP well-controlled; no medication changes at this time
- Home blood pressure monitoring recommended

3. HYPERLIPIDEMIA:
- INCREASE Atorvastatin to 80 mg PO daily (was on lower dose per discharge papers)
- Goal LDL <70 mg/dL given CAD risk factors (prior stroke, CHF)
- Repeat lipid panel in 3 months to reassess response to increased statin dose
- Continue dietary modifications (low saturated fat, low cholesterol diet)

4. ISCHEMIC STROKE PREVENTION:
- Continue current antithrombotic therapy as prescribed
- No changes at this time

5. OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNEA:
- Continue CPAP therapy with current settings (12 cmH2O, full face mask)
- Continue nightly use as tolerated
- Patient advised NO indication for supplemental home oxygen at this time
- CPAP device to be serviced per manufacturer recommendations
- Follow-up with sleep medicine if any changes in symptoms or compliance

6. FOLLOW-UP:
- Return to cardiology clinic in 3 months (approximately 06/10/2026)
- Lipid panel to be drawn prior to or at 3-month visit
- Echocardiogram to be scheduled for 6 months post-visit
- Patient to notify office of any chest pain, severe dyspnea, weight gain, new symptoms, or medication side effects
- Office phone number provided for urgent concerns

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PATIENT EDUCATION:

Ms. Valadez-Nucleus was counseled on:
- Importance of continued medication compliance
- Low-sodium diet and fluid restriction
- Daily weight monitoring and reporting significant changes
- Warning signs of CHF exacerbation
- Continued CPAP use for sleep apnea management
- Importance of lipid control in cardiac and stroke prevention
- Activity level appropriate for her current status (no strenuous activity without clearance)

Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and agreed with recommendations.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CLINICAL IMPRESSION:

This is a 55-year-old female with systolic CHF (EF 35-40%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and prior ischemic stroke who presents for post-hospitalization follow-up 4 weeks after discharge for CHF exacerbation. She has made an excellent clinical recovery with marked improvement in dyspnea and resolution of edema. She demonstrates outstanding compliance with medical therapy, dietary recommendations, and CPAP management. Her blood pressure is well-controlled. Lipid panel shows improvement but LDL remains above goal, warranting intensification of statin therapy. She will continue current medications with the addition of increased Atorvastatin dosing, and will follow up in clinic in 3 months.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Sarah Chen, MD
Cardiologist
Austin Heart & Lung Specialists

Time: [Time of visit]

            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Initial Assessment (15:30):</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>General: Alert, oriented, anxious, in moderate respiratory distress, speaking in short sentences</paragraph>
            <paragraph>HEENT: Facial angioedema noted, lips swollen, tongue mildly edematous</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Neck: No JVD, trachea midline, no lymphadenopathy</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmurs</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Pulmonary: Tachypneic with use of accessory muscles, diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally, decreased air entry, no crackles</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Skin: Diffuse urticarial rash covering trunk, extremities, and face.</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normal bowel sounds, no hepatosplenomegaly</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Extremities: No cyanosis or clubbing, warm and well-perfused, 2+ pulses</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, anxious affect, no focal deficits</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Reassessment (16:15 - Post-treatment):</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>General: Improved respiratory status, no longer in distress, speaking in full sentences</paragraph>
            <paragraph>HEENT: Facial swelling improved, lips less swollen, tongue normal size</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Pulmonary: Respiratory rate normalized, minimal scattered wheezes, improved air entry</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Skin: Urticaria significantly improved but still visible, less erythematous, decreased pruritis</paragraph>

CARDIOLOGY VISIT NOTE

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
Age/Sex: 55F
Date of Visit: 12/02/2025
Provider: Dr. Sarah Chen
Visit Type: Routine Follow-up

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Patient presents for routine cardiology follow-up. She reports overall stable cardiac symptoms but notes a mild increase in exertional dyspnea over the past 2-3 weeks, which she attributes to increased sodium intake during the holiday season. Denies chest pain, orthopnea, or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. Patient reports good compliance with CPAP therapy. Weight has increased 4 lbs since last visit (current weight 173 lbs). On examination, mild bilateral ankle edema is noted.

VITAL SIGNS AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Blood pressure 136/84 mmHg, heart rate 76 bpm (regular), SpO2 95% on room air. EKG shows normal sinus rhythm at rate 76 with stable left ventricular hypertrophy, unchanged from prior studies.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
Assessment: (1) Congestive heart failure with mild volume overload, likely secondary to dietary sodium indiscretion, currently not requiring diuretic therapy but will monitor closely; (2) Hypertension, slightly above goal on current regimen; (3) Hyperlipidemia, lipid panel ordered. Patient counseled extensively on strict sodium restriction to help resolve current volume status. Lipid panel has been ordered for 12/30/2025. Transthoracic echocardiogram scheduled for January 2026 for interval assessment. Will consider initiation of diuretic therapy if symptoms worsen. Patient instructed to return for follow-up in 4-6 weeks or sooner if experiencing worsening dyspnea, chest pain, or increased edema.

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph>32-year-old female presented to ED via EMS at 15:30 with acute onset allergic reaction. Patient reports eating shrimp scampi at local restaurant approximately 20 minutes prior to symptom onset. Within 5-10 minutes of eating, developed generalized urticaria, throat tightness, and difficulty breathing. Friend called 911 immediately.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>EMS arrived within 8 minutes and found patient in moderate distress with audible wheezing, tachypneic, diffuse hives, and facial angioedema. Patient was alert and oriented but anxious. EMS administered Epinephrine 0.3mg IM to right thigh at 15:20 with some improvement in breathing. IV access obtained en route. Patient transported to ED with lights and sirens, arrival at 15:30.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Patient denies prior history of shellfish allergy. Reports eating shrimp "many times before" without issue. Last ate shrimp approximately 6 months ago without problems. Denies recent illness, new medications, or other new exposures. Takes no daily medications. No history of asthma or prior anaphylaxis.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Review of systems: Denies chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain. Endorses severe anxiety and feeling of "impending doom." Denies loss of consciousness or confusion.</paragraph>
          </text>

CARDIOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE

PATIENT NAME: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
DATE OF BIRTH: <UNKNOWN - Age 55 years>
SEX: Female
DATE OF CONSULTATION: February 10, 2026
LOCATION: Austin Memorial Hospital
CONSULTING PHYSICIAN: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD
ADMITTING PHYSICIAN: <UNKNOWN>

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

REASON FOR CONSULT:
Management of acute decompensated heart failure (ADHF) with CHF exacerbation. Patient admitted 02/09/2026.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
The patient is a 55-year-old female admitted to Austin Memorial Hospital on February 9, 2026, for acute decompensated heart failure exacerbation. She presents with clinical evidence of volume overload and pulmonary congestion. Recent transthoracic echocardiography obtained on January 10, 2026, demonstrated significant systolic dysfunction with LVEF 35-40%, moderate left ventricular dilation, moderate mitral regurgitation (MR), and moderate left atrial (LA) enlargement.

Current laboratory studies reveal:
• NT-proBNP: 132 pg/mL
• High-sensitivity Troponin: Trending 2-6 ng/L (below myocardial infarction cutoff)
• C-reactive protein (CRP): 12.4 mg/dL

Chest X-ray findings show bilateral pleural effusions consistent with pulmonary edema.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
• Hypertension
• Hyperlipidemia
• Prior ischemic stroke
• Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA) - currently on CPAP therapy

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
• Aspirin 81 mg daily
• Atorvastatin 40 mg daily
• Clopidogrel 75 mg daily
• Lisinopril 10 mg daily
• NOTE: No current diuretic therapy on admission

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ASSESSMENT:

1. ACUTE DECOMPENSATED HEART FAILURE (ADHF)
- Systolic dysfunction with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF): LVEF 35-40%
- Pulmonary and peripheral volume overload with bilateral pleural effusions
- Secondary mitral regurgitation contributing to hemodynamic compromise
- Left atrial enlargement likely secondary to chronic mitral insufficiency

2. ETIOLOGY CONSIDERATIONS:
- Prior ischemic stroke history warrants consideration of ischemic cardiomyopathy
- Current troponin elevation (2-6 ng/L) is modest and trending; not diagnostic of acute MI
- Elevated NT-proBNP (132 pg/mL) consistent with decompensated state
- Mild inflammatory marker elevation (CRP 12.4)

3. MEDICATION OPTIMIZATION GAPS:
- No diuretic agent currently prescribed; contributing to volume overload
- ACE inhibitor dosed at 10 mg (potentially subtherapeutic)
- Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) appropriate given prior stroke history

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

RECOMMENDATIONS:

ACUTE MANAGEMENT:
1. Initiate intravenous diuresis targeting net negative 1.0-1.5 L/day to achieve euvolemic state
- Monitor urine output, daily weights, and vital signs closely
- Adjust IV diuretic dosing based on clinical response and renal function

2. Continue current ACE inhibitor therapy (Lisinopril 10 mg daily)
- Consider uptitration once acute decompensation resolves and renal function stabilized

TRANSITION TO OUTPATIENT REGIMEN:
3. Transition to oral Furosemide 20 mg daily when patient achieves euvolemic state
- Reassess diuretic requirement during outpatient follow-up

SUPPORTIVE CARE:
4. Implement strict 2 L/day fluid restriction
5. Implement low-sodium diet (<2 g/day sodium)
6. Daily weight monitoring; report gains >2-3 lbs to primary care provider

MONITORING:
7. Repeat echocardiography in 3-6 months to reassess LVEF and degree of mitral regurgitation
8. Monitor renal function and electrolytes closely during diuretic initiation and titration
9. Continue CPAP compliance monitoring for OSA management

FOLLOW-UP:
10. Outpatient cardiology follow-up appointment to be scheduled 4 weeks post-discharge
- Assess tolerance of oral diuretic regimen
- Evaluate for need for additional heart failure medications (beta-blocker, aldosterone antagonist)
- Consider device therapy evaluation (ICD/CRT) based on LVEF persistently <35% and NYHA class

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CLINICAL IMPRESSION:
Ms. Valadez-Nucleus presents with acute decompensated heart failure secondary to systolic dysfunction with reduced ejection fraction. Her current presentation is consistent with acute volume overload with pulmonary and pleural involvement. Urgent diuresis with transition to oral regimen and strict dietary/fluid management should facilitate clinical improvement. Comprehensive outpatient cardiology evaluation will be necessary to optimize her chronic heart failure regimen and assess candidacy for advanced therapies.

We appreciate the opportunity to participate in her care.

Respectfully submitted,

HOSPITAL DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS - CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE (CHF) EXACERBATION

MONITORING YOUR HEALTH AT HOME:
It is very important to monitor your fluid status daily. Please weigh yourself every morning before eating and use the same scale each time. Write down your weight each day. Call us immediately if your weight increases by more than 2 pounds in a single day or more than 5 pounds in a week, as this may indicate fluid buildup that needs attention. Additionally, elevate your legs when sitting down to help reduce swelling. Be sure to continue using your CPAP machine every night to treat your sleep apnea, as this helps your heart work better while you sleep.

DIET, FLUIDS, AND MEDICATIONS:
Follow a low-sodium diet with less than 2,000 mg (2 grams) of salt per day to prevent fluid retention. This means limiting canned foods, processed meats, and adding salt to your meals. Restrict your total fluid intake to 2 liters (about 8 cups) per day, including water, juice, and other beverages. Take all your medications exactly as prescribed. You have a new medication called Furosemide 20 mg that you will take once daily—this is a water pill that helps remove extra fluid from your body. Do not skip doses or adjust your medications without talking to your doctor first.

WHEN TO SEEK IMMEDIATE HELP:
Call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room right away if you experience worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, severe dizziness, or a sudden increase in swelling in your legs or abdomen. These could be signs of a serious problem that needs urgent care. Schedule a follow-up appointment with your cardiologist, Dr. Sarah Chen, for 4 weeks from now (March 10, 2026). If you have any questions about these instructions, please call our heart failure clinic before you leave the hospital today.

            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">DIAGNOSIS: Anaphylaxis (Severe Allergic Reaction) to Shellfish</content></paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">WHAT HAPPENED:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>You had a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction called anaphylaxis after eating shrimp. This caused difficulty breathing, hives all over your body, and facial swelling. You were treated with two doses of epinephrine (adrenaline), antihistamines, steroids, and breathing treatments. You responded well to treatment.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">ALLERGY AVOIDANCE - CRITICAL:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">NEVER eat shellfish or crustaceans again</content> - including shrimp, lobster, crab, crayfish, prawns</item>
              <item>Read ALL food labels carefully - shellfish can be hidden in soups, sauces, Asian dishes, seafood restaurants</item>
              <item>Be cautious at seafood restaurants - high risk of cross-contamination even with non-shellfish dishes</item>
              <item>Inform all restaurants about your SEVERE shellfish allergy</item>
              <item>Consider wearing medical alert bracelet</item>
              <item>Inform all healthcare providers about this allergy</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">YOUR EPIPEN - LIFE-SAVING INFORMATION:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">ALWAYS carry TWO EpiPens with you at all times</content></item>
              <item>Use EpiPen immediately if you experience ANY of these symptoms:
                <list>
                  <item>Difficulty breathing or throat tightness</item>
                  <item>Widespread hives or rapid swelling</item>
                  <item>Dizziness or feeling faint</item>
                  <item>Sense that "something is very wrong"</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>HOW TO USE EPIPEN:
                <list>
                  <item>Remove from carrier tube</item>
                  <item>Hold firmly with orange tip pointing down</item>
                  <item>Remove blue safety cap by pulling straight up</item>
                  <item>Place orange tip against outer thigh (can go through clothing)</item>
                  <item>Push down hard until you hear a click</item>
                  <item>Hold in place for 3 seconds</item>
                  <item>Remove and massage injection site</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">After using EpiPen: ALWAYS call 911 and go to emergency department</content></item>
              <item>You may need second dose if symptoms don't improve in 5-10 minutes</item>
              <item>Store at room temperature (not in car or refrigerator)</item>
              <item>Check expiration date monthly and get refills</item>
              <item>Teach family/friends how to use EpiPen</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">MEDICATIONS:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Take prednisone 60mg daily for 3 days (prevents late reaction)</item>
              <item>Take famotidine 20mg twice daily for 3 days</item>
              <item>Take Benadryl 50mg as needed for residual itching</item>
              <item>Complete all medications even if feeling better</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">WARNING - BIPHASIC REACTION:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>In 20% of anaphylaxis cases, symptoms can return 4-12 hours later even after initial improvement. This is called a biphasic reaction. For this reason:</paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Have someone stay with you tonight</item>
              <item>Keep EpiPen within arm's reach at all times</item>
              <item>Avoid alcohol and strenuous activity for 24 hours</item>
              <item>Do not hesitate to use EpiPen and call 911 if ANY symptoms return</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">RETURN TO EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT IMMEDIATELY IF:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Difficulty breathing or swallowing</item>
              <item>Chest tightness or throat closing sensation</item>
              <item>New or worsening hives</item>
              <item>Facial, lip, or tongue swelling</item>
              <item>Dizziness, lightheadedness, or feeling faint</item>
              <item>Rapid heartbeat or irregular heartbeat</item>
              <item>Severe abdominal cramping or vomiting</item>
              <item>Confusion or feeling of impending doom</item>
              <item>ANY concern that allergic reaction is returning</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">FOLLOW-UP APPOINTMENTS:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">Allergist appointment within 2 weeks</content> (referral placed - they will call you)
                <list>
                  <item>Comprehensive allergy testing</item>
                  <item>Evaluate for other potential food allergies</item>
                  <item>Discuss cross-reactivity with other seafood</item>
                  <item>Long-term management plan</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>Primary care doctor within 1 week for follow-up</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">ACTIVITY:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Rest today and tomorrow</item>
              <item>Avoid strenuous exercise for 24-48 hours</item>
              <item>Benadryl may cause drowsiness - do not drive or operate machinery</item>
              <item>Can return to normal activities once feeling completely better</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">QUESTIONS ANSWERED:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient verbalizes understanding of:</paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Need to avoid all shellfish permanently</item>
              <item>How and when to use EpiPen</item>
              <item>Importance of carrying two EpiPens at all times</item>
              <item>Warning signs requiring immediate return to ED</item>
              <item>Risk of biphasic reaction in next 12 hours</item>
              <item>Need for allergist follow-up</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Patient received written discharge instructions and demonstrates proper EpiPen technique.</content></paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Discharge time: 18:30</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Discharge condition: Stable, symptoms resolved</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Discharged to: Home with responsible adult</paragraph>
          </text>

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">15:30 - ED Arrival and Initial Assessment:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient arrived via EMS with acute anaphylaxis following shellfish ingestion. EMS had already administered first dose of Epinephrine IM (0.3mg) at 15:20 with partial response. Upon arrival, patient in moderate respiratory distress with persistent wheezing, urticaria, and facial angioedema. Initial vital signs concerning for continued anaphylaxis with tachycardia (HR 112), tachypnea (RR 26), and borderline hypoxia (SpO2 93% on RA).</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">15:35-15:50 - Acute Management:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient immediately placed on continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry. IV access already established by EMS. Given persistent symptoms despite EMS epinephrine, SECOND dose of Epinephrine 0.3mg IM administered to left thigh at 15:35. Patient placed on 4L O2 via nasal cannula with improvement in SpO2 to 96%. Administered IV Diphenhydramine 50mg, IV Famotidine 20mg. Normal saline bolus initiated for volume resuscitation given relative hypotension.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">16:00-16:30 - Continued Treatment:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient showing gradual improvement but continued to have significant wheezing. Labs sent including serum tryptase (drawn at 1 hour post-symptom onset). Chest x-ray obtained to rule out aspiration or other complications - negative. ECG showed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes. Repeat albuterol treatment at 16:30 resolved remaining bronchospasm.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">16:30-18:00 - Observation Period:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient observed for biphasic reaction (can occur 4-12 hours after initial reaction in 20% of cases). Vital signs stabilized with normalization of HR and RR. Oxygen weaned to 2L then room air with maintained SpO2 99%. Urticaria and angioedema markedly improved. Patient able to ambulate without distress. Serum tryptase returned elevated at 18.5 mcg/L, confirming anaphylaxis diagnosis. Patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout 3-hour observation period with no recurrence of symptoms.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">18:00-18:30 - Discharge Planning:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>After minimum 3-hour observation period with complete resolution of respiratory symptoms and stable vital signs, patient deemed safe for discharge. Extensive education provided regarding shellfish allergy, avoidance strategies, and recognition of anaphylaxis symptoms. Patient understands need to avoid ALL shellfish and crustaceans. Prescribed EpiPen auto-injector with detailed training on proper use. Instructed to return immediately if any symptoms recur. Allergist referral placed for comprehensive allergy testing and long-term management. Patient verbalizes understanding of all discharge instructions and demonstrates proper EpiPen technique.</paragraph>
          </text>

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph>32-year-old female presented to ED via EMS at 15:30 with acute onset allergic reaction. Patient reports eating shrimp scampi at local restaurant approximately 20 minutes prior to symptom onset. Within 5-10 minutes of eating, developed generalized urticaria, throat tightness, and difficulty breathing. Friend called 911 immediately.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>EMS arrived within 8 minutes and found patient in moderate distress with audible wheezing, tachypneic, diffuse hives, and facial angioedema. Patient was alert and oriented but anxious. EMS administered Epinephrine 0.3mg IM to right thigh at 15:20 with some improvement in breathing. IV access obtained en route. Patient transported to ED with lights and sirens, arrival at 15:30.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Patient denies prior history of shellfish allergy. Reports eating shrimp "many times before" without issue. Last ate shrimp approximately 6 months ago without problems. Denies recent illness, new medications, or other new exposures. Takes no daily medications. No history of asthma or prior anaphylaxis.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Review of systems: Denies chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain. Endorses severe anxiety and feeling of "impending doom." Denies loss of consciousness or confusion.</paragraph>
          </text>
<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">Data Unknown</text>

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">DIAGNOSIS: Anaphylaxis (Severe Allergic Reaction) to Shellfish</content></paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">WHAT HAPPENED:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>You had a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction called anaphylaxis after eating shrimp. This caused difficulty breathing, hives all over your body, and facial swelling. You were treated with two doses of epinephrine (adrenaline), antihistamines, steroids, and breathing treatments. You responded well to treatment.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">ALLERGY AVOIDANCE - CRITICAL:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">NEVER eat shellfish or crustaceans again</content> - including shrimp, lobster, crab, crayfish, prawns</item>
              <item>Read ALL food labels carefully - shellfish can be hidden in soups, sauces, Asian dishes, seafood restaurants</item>
              <item>Be cautious at seafood restaurants - high risk of cross-contamination even with non-shellfish dishes</item>
              <item>Inform all restaurants about your SEVERE shellfish allergy</item>
              <item>Consider wearing medical alert bracelet</item>
              <item>Inform all healthcare providers about this allergy</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">YOUR EPIPEN - LIFE-SAVING INFORMATION:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">ALWAYS carry TWO EpiPens with you at all times</content></item>
              <item>Use EpiPen immediately if you experience ANY of these symptoms:
                <list>
                  <item>Difficulty breathing or throat tightness</item>
                  <item>Widespread hives or rapid swelling</item>
                  <item>Dizziness or feeling faint</item>
                  <item>Sense that "something is very wrong"</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>HOW TO USE EPIPEN:
                <list>
                  <item>Remove from carrier tube</item>
                  <item>Hold firmly with orange tip pointing down</item>
                  <item>Remove blue safety cap by pulling straight up</item>
                  <item>Place orange tip against outer thigh (can go through clothing)</item>
                  <item>Push down hard until you hear a click</item>
                  <item>Hold in place for 3 seconds</item>
                  <item>Remove and massage injection site</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">After using EpiPen: ALWAYS call 911 and go to emergency department</content></item>
              <item>You may need second dose if symptoms don't improve in 5-10 minutes</item>
              <item>Store at room temperature (not in car or refrigerator)</item>
              <item>Check expiration date monthly and get refills</item>
              <item>Teach family/friends how to use EpiPen</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">MEDICATIONS:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Take prednisone 60mg daily for 3 days (prevents late reaction)</item>
              <item>Take famotidine 20mg twice daily for 3 days</item>
              <item>Take Benadryl 50mg as needed for residual itching</item>
              <item>Complete all medications even if feeling better</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">WARNING - BIPHASIC REACTION:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>In 20% of anaphylaxis cases, symptoms can return 4-12 hours later even after initial improvement. This is called a biphasic reaction. For this reason:</paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Have someone stay with you tonight</item>
              <item>Keep EpiPen within arm's reach at all times</item>
              <item>Avoid alcohol and strenuous activity for 24 hours</item>
              <item>Do not hesitate to use EpiPen and call 911 if ANY symptoms return</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">RETURN TO EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT IMMEDIATELY IF:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Difficulty breathing or swallowing</item>
              <item>Chest tightness or throat closing sensation</item>
              <item>New or worsening hives</item>
              <item>Facial, lip, or tongue swelling</item>
              <item>Dizziness, lightheadedness, or feeling faint</item>
              <item>Rapid heartbeat or irregular heartbeat</item>
              <item>Severe abdominal cramping or vomiting</item>
              <item>Confusion or feeling of impending doom</item>
              <item>ANY concern that allergic reaction is returning</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">FOLLOW-UP APPOINTMENTS:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item><content styleCode="Bold">Allergist appointment within 2 weeks</content> (referral placed - they will call you)
                <list>
                  <item>Comprehensive allergy testing</item>
                  <item>Evaluate for other potential food allergies</item>
                  <item>Discuss cross-reactivity with other seafood</item>
                  <item>Long-term management plan</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>Primary care doctor within 1 week for follow-up</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">ACTIVITY:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Rest today and tomorrow</item>
              <item>Avoid strenuous exercise for 24-48 hours</item>
              <item>Benadryl may cause drowsiness - do not drive or operate machinery</item>
              <item>Can return to normal activities once feeling completely better</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">QUESTIONS ANSWERED:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient verbalizes understanding of:</paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Need to avoid all shellfish permanently</item>
              <item>How and when to use EpiPen</item>
              <item>Importance of carrying two EpiPens at all times</item>
              <item>Warning signs requiring immediate return to ED</item>
              <item>Risk of biphasic reaction in next 12 hours</item>
              <item>Need for allergist follow-up</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Patient received written discharge instructions and demonstrates proper EpiPen technique.</content></paragraph>
            
            <paragraph>Discharge time: 18:30</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Discharge condition: Stable, symptoms resolved</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Discharged to: Home with responsible adult</paragraph>
          </text>

Medication Dose Route Frequency Duration Instructions
Epinephrine Auto-Injector (EpiPen) 0.3 mg IM (intramuscular) As needed for allergic reaction CARRY AT ALL TIMES CRITICAL: Use immediately if experiencing difficulty breathing, throat tightness, or widespread hives. Inject into outer thigh. Call 911 immediately after use. ALWAYS CARRY TWO EpiPens. Check expiration date monthly.
Prednisone 60 mg Oral Once daily 3 days Take in morning with food. Prevents late-phase allergic reaction. Complete full course even if feeling better. May cause increased appetite, difficulty sleeping, mood changes.
Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg Oral Every 6 hours as needed 48 hours For residual itching or hives. May cause drowsiness - do not drive or operate machinery. Keep on hand for minor allergic reactions (but use EpiPen for severe symptoms).
Famotidine (Pepcid) 20 mg Oral Twice daily 3 days H2-blocker to reduce allergic inflammation. Take morning and evening.
MEDICATION SAFETY: EpiPen is life-saving medication - do NOT wait to use if experiencing severe allergic symptoms After using EpiPen, you MUST go to emergency department even if feeling better Store EpiPen at room temperature, avoid extreme heat or cold Check liquid in EpiPen window - should be clear, not discolored Get refills before expiration date Inform family, friends, coworkers about allergy and how to help if needed

CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION PROCEDURE REPORT

PATIENT NAME: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
PATIENT AGE/GENDER: 55F
DATE OF PROCEDURE: 03/22/2026
INSTITUTION: Austin Memorial Hospital
ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Raj Patel, MD (Interventional Cardiologist)
PROCEDURE: Left Heart Catheterization with Diagnostic Coronary Angiography

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

INDICATION FOR PROCEDURE:

55-year-old female with ischemic cardiomyopathy (ejection fraction 35-40% on transthoracic echocardiogram dated 01/10/2026) presenting with evaluation of coronary anatomy in the setting of:
• Progressive worsening of heart failure symptoms despite optimal medical therapy
• History of ischemic stroke (prior neurological event)
• Known hyperlipidemia
• Prior inferior wall fixed perfusion defect on nuclear stress testing
• Reduced left ventricular systolic function
Patient was deemed appropriate for left heart catheterization to establish coronary anatomy and assess potential revascularization candidacy.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PROCEDURE DETAILS:

The procedure was performed under local anesthesia with conscious sedation. All equipment was sterilized and standard sterile technique was maintained throughout. Standard hemodynamic monitoring with continuous electrocardiography and pulse oximetry was employed. The patient tolerated the procedure well without immediate complications.

Access Site: Right radial artery via percutaneous puncture using 6 French (6F) access.

Catheters Utilized:
• 6F TIG 3.5 diagnostic catheter for left main coronary artery engagement
• 6F JL4 diagnostic catheter for right coronary artery engagement
• 6F pigtail catheter for left ventricular pressure monitoring and left ventriculography

Contrast Media: Iso-osmolar contrast medium (IOCM) utilized. Total contrast volume: 120 mL.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

VASCULAR ACCESS AND TECHNIQUE:

Right radial artery puncture was performed using standard Seldinger technique. A 6 French arterial sheath was successfully placed without difficulty. Catheter exchanges were performed smoothly without complications. Hemostasis was achieved with radial band compression at the conclusion of the procedure. Distal radial and ulnar pulses were intact post-procedure with adequate perfusion to the right hand.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

HEMODYNAMIC FINDINGS:

Left Ventricular Pressures:
• Left Ventricular End-Diastolic Pressure (LVEDP): 22 mmHg (ELEVATED)
- Elevation consistent with left ventricular dysfunction and diastolic filling impairment in the setting of ischemic cardiomyopathy

Aortic Pressures:
• Systolic: 130 mmHg
• Diastolic: 78 mmHg
• Mean: 95 mmHg

Interpretation: Hemodynamic profile demonstrates elevated filling pressures consistent with decompensated congestive heart failure. Systemic blood pressure is well-controlled on current antihypertensive regimen.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CORONARY ANGIOGRAPHY FINDINGS:

LEFT MAIN CORONARY ARTERY (LMCA):
• Patent
• No significant stenosis
• Normal caliber and branch distribution
• Gives rise to LAD and LCx systems without obstruction

LEFT ANTERIOR DESCENDING (LAD) ARTERY:
• Origin: Normal from left main coronary artery
• Caliber: Adequate
• Course: Normal anterior interventricular distribution
• Findings: Diffuse mild-to-moderate atherosclerotic changes throughout the vessel
• Significant stenosis: 50% narrowing in the mid-LAD segment
• Branches: Diagonal branches patent with mild disease
• Flow: TIMI 3 (normal)
• Assessment: Moderate disease not immediately amenable to percutaneous intervention at this time

LEFT CIRCUMFLEX (LCx) ARTERY:
• Origin: Normal from left main coronary artery
• Caliber: Normal
• Course: Normal distribution along left ventricular lateral wall
• Findings: 40% stenosis in the proximal segment
• Mild diffuse atherosclerotic disease noted throughout the distal vessel
• Obtuse marginal branches: Patent
• Left ventricular branches: Patent
• Flow: TIMI 3
• Assessment: Mild-to-moderate disease, not flow-limiting at present

RIGHT CORONARY ARTERY (RCA):
• Origin: Normal
• Proximal segment: Patent with mild atherosclerotic disease
• Mid-segment: 100% CHRONIC TOTAL OCCLUSION (CTO)
• Distal segment: Not visualized anterograde
• Collateral circulation: Well-developed left-to-right collaterals from LAD (septal perforators and diagonal branches) providing retrograde filling of distal RCA and posterolateral branches
• Assessment: CTO consistent with remote (old) inferior wall myocardial infarction with good collateral support

Summary of Coronary Disease:
• Two-vessel disease: LAD (50% mid-segment) and RCA (100% CTO)
• LCx: Mild-to-moderate (40% proximal) disease
• No lesions amenable to percutaneous coronary intervention at this time
• Pattern consistent with prior inferior myocardial infarction with collateral development

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

LEFT VENTRICULOGRAPHY FINDINGS:

Technique: 8 mL of contrast injected in RAO 30° view with simultaneous biplane imaging.

Ventricular Size:
• Left ventricle is mildly enlarged
• End-systolic volume: Increased
• End-diastolic volume: Increased

Systolic Function:
• Ejection Fraction: Approximately 35% (severely reduced)
• Global contractility: Markedly decreased
• Regional wall motion abnormalities noted

Wall Motion Abnormalities:
• Inferior wall: Hypokinesis
• Inferolateral wall: Hypokinesis
• Anterior wall: Preserved
• Anteromedial wall: Preserved
• Apex: Preserved
• Pattern consistent with prior inferior myocardial infarction

Valve Function:
• Mitral valve: Mild-to-moderate regurgitation noted
• Aortic valve: No stenosis or regurgitation
• Tricuspid valve: Appears normal

Diastolic Function:
• Delayed filling pattern consistent with impaired relaxation

Overall Assessment: Severely reduced left ventricular systolic function with ejection fraction of approximately 35%, regional hypokinesis in the inferior and inferolateral distribution consistent with prior myocardial infarction, and mild-to-moderate functional mitral regurgitation secondary to ventricular remodeling and dilation.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

COMPLICATIONS:

No procedural complications encountered. The patient had:
• No contrast-induced nephropathy
• No coronary dissection
• No distal embolization
• No bleeding complications
• No vascular complications
• No arrhythmias requiring intervention

Hemostasis: Radial band hemostasis successfully applied at procedure conclusion with appropriate compression. Post-procedure assessment confirmed intact distal perfusion.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS:

1. CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE: Two-vessel coronary artery disease identified with a 100% chronic total occlusion of the mid-right coronary artery (consistent with prior inferior myocardial infarction) and a 50% stenosis of the mid-left anterior descending artery with diffuse mild-to-moderate atherosclerosis. The left circumflex demonstrates a 40% proximal stenosis with mild diffuse disease. The left main coronary artery remains unobstructed.

2. REVASCULARIZATION ASSESSMENT: At this time, the patient's coronary lesions are not amenable to percutaneous coronary intervention. The RCA CTO is chronic with well-developed collateral circulation. The LAD stenosis is moderate and does not significantly limit flow. No acute indication for revascularization is present. The patient should continue on optimal medical therapy for secondary prevention and heart failure management.

3. LEFT VENTRICULAR FUNCTION: Severely reduced left ventricular systolic function with ejection fraction of approximately 35%. Regional wall motion abnormality consistent with prior myocardial infarction in the inferior distribution. Mild-to-moderate functional mitral regurgitation secondary to remodeling.

4. HEMODYNAMICS: Elevated left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (22 mmHg) consistent with diastolic dysfunction and congestive heart failure. Aortic pressures are well-controlled.

5. RECOMMENDATIONS:
a) Continue optimal medical therapy including:
• ACE inhibitor or ARB for cardioprotection
• Beta-blocker for symptom control and mortality benefit
• Aldosterone antagonist if indicated
• High-intensity statin for lipid management
• Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin)
• Diuretics as needed for volume management

b) URGENT CONSIDERATION FOR CRT-D EVALUATION: Given the significantly reduced ejection fraction (≤35%) in the setting of ischemic cardiomyopathy, the patient should be evaluated for cardiac resynchronization therapy with defibrillator (CRT-D) capability. This may provide symptomatic improvement and potential mortality benefit. Recommend formal electrophysiology consultation and echocardiographic assessment of QRS duration and mechanical dyssynchrony.

c) Continued risk factor modification including hypertension control, lipid management, smoking cessation (if applicable), and diabetes control.

d) Close cardiology follow-up with repeat echocardiography in 3 months to assess response to medical therapy and suitability for CRT-D.

e) Neurology follow-up for management of prior ischemic stroke.

6. PROGNOSIS: Without revascularization, the patient's prognosis is primarily dependent upon ventricular function and response to medical therapy. CRT-D consideration is important given the severely reduced EF and potential for sudden cardiac death.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Immediate Actions:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Complete 3-day course of prednisone to prevent biphasic reaction</item>
              <item>ALWAYS carry two EpiPen auto-injectors</item>
              <item>Complete avoidance of all shellfish and crustaceans</item>
              <item>Obtain medical alert bracelet identifying shellfish allergy</item>
              <item>Educate family/friends about allergy and EpiPen use</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Follow-up Care:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Allergist appointment within 2 weeks (URGENT)
                <list>
                  <item>Comprehensive allergy evaluation</item>
                  <item>Skin prick testing or specific IgE testing</item>
                  <item>Evaluate cross-reactivity with other seafood (fish, mollusks)</item>
                  <item>Consider testing for other potential food allergies</item>
                  <item>Provide written anaphylaxis action plan</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>Primary care physician within 1 week
                <list>
                  <item>Review ED course and medications</item>
                  <item>Ensure EpiPen prescription filled</item>
                  <item>Update problem list and allergy list</item>
                  <item>Coordinate with allergist</item>
                </list>
              </item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Long-term Management:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Lifelong shellfish avoidance</item>
              <item>Always carry epinephrine auto-injectors (replace before expiration)</item>
              <item>Wear medical identification jewelry</item>
              <item>Inform all healthcare providers of severe allergy</item>
              <item>Educate about cross-contamination risks in restaurants</item>
              <item>Annual allergist follow-up recommended</item>
              <item>Consider food allergy action plan for workplace/school</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Biphasic Reaction Monitoring:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Remain under observation by responsible adult for 24 hours</item>
              <item>Peak risk for biphasic reaction is 4-12 hours post-initial reaction</item>
              <item>Keep EpiPen immediately accessible</item>
              <item>Use EpiPen and call 911 if ANY symptoms recur</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Patient Education Completed:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Recognition of anaphylaxis symptoms</item>
              <item>EpiPen administration technique (demonstrated correctly)</item>
              <item>Importance of calling 911 after EpiPen use</item>
              <item>Shellfish avoidance strategies</item>
              <item>Cross-contamination awareness</item>
              <item>Biphasic reaction risk and monitoring</item>
              <item>Return precautions</item>
              <item>Written discharge instructions provided</item>
            </list>
          </text>

CARDIOLOGY VISIT NOTE

PATIENT: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus | DOB: 08/19/1970 | Age: 55F
DATE OF VISIT: 08/19/2025
PROVIDER: Dr. Sarah Chen

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Patient is a 55-year-old female presenting for routine cardiology follow-up. She reports feeling well overall and is tolerating her medications without difficulty. She denies chest pain. She notes mild dyspnea on exertion with climbing two flights of stairs. Patient is compliant with CPAP therapy. Vitals today: BP 130/80 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, SpO2 96% on room air.

CLINICAL FINDINGS & IMAGING REVIEW:
Transthoracic echocardiogram from 08/16/2025 was reviewed and shows LVEF 40-45% with mild left ventricular dilation, Grade II diastolic dysfunction, mild-to-moderate mitral regurgitation, and mild left atrial enlargement. Right ventricular function remains normal. Findings are slightly improved compared to prior study. EKG today demonstrates normal sinus rhythm with left ventricular hypertrophy and nonspecific ST-T wave changes, stable from prior.

ASSESSMENT & PLAN:
1. Congestive Heart Failure: LVEF 40-45% with mild improvement from baseline. Patient symptomatically doing well with minimal dyspnea. Continue current medical regimen.
2. Hypertension: Well-controlled on current antihypertensive therapy.
3. Follow-up labs: Repeat lipid panel in December 2025.
4. Follow-up appointment: 3-4 months. Patient to call if any worsening symptoms, chest pain, or increased dyspnea develop.
<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">Data Unknown</text>
<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">There are no current procedures at the time of this document's creation.</text>

POST-PROCEDURE PLAN AND RECOMMENDATIONS

Following diagnostic left heart catheterization revealing chronic right coronary artery occlusion with prior inferior myocardial infarction, moderate LAD stenosis (50%), mild left circumflex disease, and severely reduced left ventricular ejection fraction of 35% with elevated left ventricular end-diastolic pressure, the patient will continue guideline-directed medical therapy for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction including ACE inhibitor, consideration for beta-blocker optimization, diuretic therapy, antiplatelet agent, and high-intensity statin. Given the ejection fraction ≤35% and NYHA functional class II-III heart failure symptoms, urgent referral has been made to cardiology heart failure and electrophysiology services for evaluation of cardiac resynchronization therapy with defibrillator (CRT-D) implantation. The patient should follow up with Dr. Sarah Chen in 2 weeks for continued management and post-catheterization assessment. Strict adherence to secondary prevention measures is recommended. The patient may resume normal activities tomorrow; the radial access site should be kept clean and dry for 48 hours to prevent infection and ensure proper hemostasis.
