SLEEP MEDICINE OFFICE VISIT NOTE

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
Age: 55F
Provider: Dr. Michael Torres
Facility: Austin Heart & Lung Specialists
Date of Visit: 04/18/2024

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Patient presents for follow-up evaluation following polysomnography performed on 03/14/2024. Study results revealed moderate-severe obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) with an Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) of 32 events per hour and a nadir oxygen saturation of 78%. Patient reports symptoms consistent with OSA including daytime somnolence and witnessed apneic episodes. Patient is interested in pursuing continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy and reports understanding of the diagnosis and treatment plan.

VITAL SIGNS AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm, oxygen saturation 95% on room air. General: Alert and oriented. ENT examination unremarkable. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
Moderate-severe obstructive sleep apnea confirmed by polysomnography. Patient educated on the pathophysiology of OSA and its relationship to her past medical history of congestive heart failure, emphasizing the cardiovascular benefits of treatment. CPAP therapy initiated at a pressure setting of 12 cmH2O with full face mask interface and ramp time set at 20 minutes to facilitate acclimatization. Comprehensive patient education provided regarding CPAP device operation, proper mask fitting techniques, and daily cleaning and maintenance protocols. Patient verbalized understanding of instructions. Follow-up appointment scheduled in 6 months to assess CPAP compliance, efficacy, and symptomatic improvement. Patient counseled to contact office with any questions or concerns regarding therapy.

HOSPITAL COURSE NARRATIVE

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
Age: 55 years old
MRN: <UNKNOWN>
Admission Date: 02/09/2026
Discharge Date: 02/12/2026
Admission Diagnosis: Acute Decompensated Congestive Heart Failure

HOSPITAL COURSE:

Ms. Elvira Valadez-Nucleus is a 55-year-old female with a significant past medical history of congestive heart failure (ejection fraction 35-40%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, prior ischemic stroke in 2022, and obstructive sleep apnea on CPAP therapy who presented to Austin Memorial Hospital on 02/09/2026 with acute decompensation. The patient reported progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, and bilateral lower extremity edema that had been worsening over the preceding week. On admission, vital signs revealed hypertension (BP 155/95 mmHg), tachycardia (HR 98 bpm), tachypnea (RR 24 breaths/min), mild hypoxemia (SpO2 91% on room air), and an admission weight of 176 lbs. Initial laboratory evaluation demonstrated elevated NT-proBNP at 132 pg/mL, with serial high-sensitivity troponin I values of 2 ng/L at 23:04 on 02/09 and 6 ng/L at 06:00 on 02/10, both below the 99th percentile, effectively ruling out acute myocardial infarction as a contributing etiology. C-reactive protein was elevated at 12.4 mg/L. Sputum culture yielded normal flora only. Chest radiography confirmed clinical suspicions, demonstrating cardiomegaly, bilateral pleural effusions, and pulmonary edema consistent with acute CHF exacerbation.

The patient was initiated on intravenous furosemide therapy with excellent diuretic response. Conservative measures including strict intake and output monitoring, implementation of a 2-liter daily fluid restriction, and adherence to a low-sodium diet were instituted. Cardiology consultation was obtained on 02/10/2026 with Dr. Sarah Chen, who evaluated the patient and provided comprehensive recommendations for CHF management optimization. Dr. Chen recommended a planned transition from intravenous to oral diuretic therapy as clinical stability improved.

Over the subsequent hospital course, the patient demonstrated steady clinical improvement. Dyspnea progressively resolved, bilateral lower extremity edema improved significantly, and body weight decreased from 176 lbs to 170 lbs by discharge. Oxygenation improved markedly, with SpO2 normalizing to 96% on room air prior to discharge. The patient tolerated the transition to oral diuretics without difficulty and demonstrated good compliance with all therapeutic interventions and dietary modifications.

Ms. Valadez-Nucleus was discharged on 02/12/2026 in stable condition with improved clinical status. Discharge medications included the addition of Furosemide 20 mg orally daily to her existing chronic heart failure regimen. The patient was discharged with explicit instructions regarding fluid and sodium restriction, and close outpatient cardiology follow-up was arranged to ensure continued optimization of her heart failure management and monitoring for recurrent decompensation.

CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION PROCEDURE REPORT

PATIENT NAME: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
PATIENT AGE/GENDER: 55F
DATE OF PROCEDURE: 03/22/2026
INSTITUTION: Austin Memorial Hospital
ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Raj Patel, MD (Interventional Cardiologist)
PROCEDURE: Left Heart Catheterization with Diagnostic Coronary Angiography

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

INDICATION FOR PROCEDURE:

55-year-old female with ischemic cardiomyopathy (ejection fraction 35-40% on transthoracic echocardiogram dated 01/10/2026) presenting with evaluation of coronary anatomy in the setting of:
• Progressive worsening of heart failure symptoms despite optimal medical therapy
• History of ischemic stroke (prior neurological event)
• Known hyperlipidemia
• Prior inferior wall fixed perfusion defect on nuclear stress testing
• Reduced left ventricular systolic function
Patient was deemed appropriate for left heart catheterization to establish coronary anatomy and assess potential revascularization candidacy.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PROCEDURE DETAILS:

The procedure was performed under local anesthesia with conscious sedation. All equipment was sterilized and standard sterile technique was maintained throughout. Standard hemodynamic monitoring with continuous electrocardiography and pulse oximetry was employed. The patient tolerated the procedure well without immediate complications.

Access Site: Right radial artery via percutaneous puncture using 6 French (6F) access.

Catheters Utilized:
• 6F TIG 3.5 diagnostic catheter for left main coronary artery engagement
• 6F JL4 diagnostic catheter for right coronary artery engagement
• 6F pigtail catheter for left ventricular pressure monitoring and left ventriculography

Contrast Media: Iso-osmolar contrast medium (IOCM) utilized. Total contrast volume: 120 mL.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

VASCULAR ACCESS AND TECHNIQUE:

Right radial artery puncture was performed using standard Seldinger technique. A 6 French arterial sheath was successfully placed without difficulty. Catheter exchanges were performed smoothly without complications. Hemostasis was achieved with radial band compression at the conclusion of the procedure. Distal radial and ulnar pulses were intact post-procedure with adequate perfusion to the right hand.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

HEMODYNAMIC FINDINGS:

Left Ventricular Pressures:
• Left Ventricular End-Diastolic Pressure (LVEDP): 22 mmHg (ELEVATED)
- Elevation consistent with left ventricular dysfunction and diastolic filling impairment in the setting of ischemic cardiomyopathy

Aortic Pressures:
• Systolic: 130 mmHg
• Diastolic: 78 mmHg
• Mean: 95 mmHg

Interpretation: Hemodynamic profile demonstrates elevated filling pressures consistent with decompensated congestive heart failure. Systemic blood pressure is well-controlled on current antihypertensive regimen.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CORONARY ANGIOGRAPHY FINDINGS:

LEFT MAIN CORONARY ARTERY (LMCA):
• Patent
• No significant stenosis
• Normal caliber and branch distribution
• Gives rise to LAD and LCx systems without obstruction

LEFT ANTERIOR DESCENDING (LAD) ARTERY:
• Origin: Normal from left main coronary artery
• Caliber: Adequate
• Course: Normal anterior interventricular distribution
• Findings: Diffuse mild-to-moderate atherosclerotic changes throughout the vessel
• Significant stenosis: 50% narrowing in the mid-LAD segment
• Branches: Diagonal branches patent with mild disease
• Flow: TIMI 3 (normal)
• Assessment: Moderate disease not immediately amenable to percutaneous intervention at this time

LEFT CIRCUMFLEX (LCx) ARTERY:
• Origin: Normal from left main coronary artery
• Caliber: Normal
• Course: Normal distribution along left ventricular lateral wall
• Findings: 40% stenosis in the proximal segment
• Mild diffuse atherosclerotic disease noted throughout the distal vessel
• Obtuse marginal branches: Patent
• Left ventricular branches: Patent
• Flow: TIMI 3
• Assessment: Mild-to-moderate disease, not flow-limiting at present

RIGHT CORONARY ARTERY (RCA):
• Origin: Normal
• Proximal segment: Patent with mild atherosclerotic disease
• Mid-segment: 100% CHRONIC TOTAL OCCLUSION (CTO)
• Distal segment: Not visualized anterograde
• Collateral circulation: Well-developed left-to-right collaterals from LAD (septal perforators and diagonal branches) providing retrograde filling of distal RCA and posterolateral branches
• Assessment: CTO consistent with remote (old) inferior wall myocardial infarction with good collateral support

Summary of Coronary Disease:
• Two-vessel disease: LAD (50% mid-segment) and RCA (100% CTO)
• LCx: Mild-to-moderate (40% proximal) disease
• No lesions amenable to percutaneous coronary intervention at this time
• Pattern consistent with prior inferior myocardial infarction with collateral development

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

LEFT VENTRICULOGRAPHY FINDINGS:

Technique: 8 mL of contrast injected in RAO 30° view with simultaneous biplane imaging.

Ventricular Size:
• Left ventricle is mildly enlarged
• End-systolic volume: Increased
• End-diastolic volume: Increased

Systolic Function:
• Ejection Fraction: Approximately 35% (severely reduced)
• Global contractility: Markedly decreased
• Regional wall motion abnormalities noted

Wall Motion Abnormalities:
• Inferior wall: Hypokinesis
• Inferolateral wall: Hypokinesis
• Anterior wall: Preserved
• Anteromedial wall: Preserved
• Apex: Preserved
• Pattern consistent with prior inferior myocardial infarction

Valve Function:
• Mitral valve: Mild-to-moderate regurgitation noted
• Aortic valve: No stenosis or regurgitation
• Tricuspid valve: Appears normal

Diastolic Function:
• Delayed filling pattern consistent with impaired relaxation

Overall Assessment: Severely reduced left ventricular systolic function with ejection fraction of approximately 35%, regional hypokinesis in the inferior and inferolateral distribution consistent with prior myocardial infarction, and mild-to-moderate functional mitral regurgitation secondary to ventricular remodeling and dilation.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

COMPLICATIONS:

No procedural complications encountered. The patient had:
• No contrast-induced nephropathy
• No coronary dissection
• No distal embolization
• No bleeding complications
• No vascular complications
• No arrhythmias requiring intervention

Hemostasis: Radial band hemostasis successfully applied at procedure conclusion with appropriate compression. Post-procedure assessment confirmed intact distal perfusion.

═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS:

1. CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE: Two-vessel coronary artery disease identified with a 100% chronic total occlusion of the mid-right coronary artery (consistent with prior inferior myocardial infarction) and a 50% stenosis of the mid-left anterior descending artery with diffuse mild-to-moderate atherosclerosis. The left circumflex demonstrates a 40% proximal stenosis with mild diffuse disease. The left main coronary artery remains unobstructed.

2. REVASCULARIZATION ASSESSMENT: At this time, the patient's coronary lesions are not amenable to percutaneous coronary intervention. The RCA CTO is chronic with well-developed collateral circulation. The LAD stenosis is moderate and does not significantly limit flow. No acute indication for revascularization is present. The patient should continue on optimal medical therapy for secondary prevention and heart failure management.

3. LEFT VENTRICULAR FUNCTION: Severely reduced left ventricular systolic function with ejection fraction of approximately 35%. Regional wall motion abnormality consistent with prior myocardial infarction in the inferior distribution. Mild-to-moderate functional mitral regurgitation secondary to remodeling.

4. HEMODYNAMICS: Elevated left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (22 mmHg) consistent with diastolic dysfunction and congestive heart failure. Aortic pressures are well-controlled.

5. RECOMMENDATIONS:
a) Continue optimal medical therapy including:
• ACE inhibitor or ARB for cardioprotection
• Beta-blocker for symptom control and mortality benefit
• Aldosterone antagonist if indicated
• High-intensity statin for lipid management
• Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin)
• Diuretics as needed for volume management

b) URGENT CONSIDERATION FOR CRT-D EVALUATION: Given the significantly reduced ejection fraction (≤35%) in the setting of ischemic cardiomyopathy, the patient should be evaluated for cardiac resynchronization therapy with defibrillator (CRT-D) capability. This may provide symptomatic improvement and potential mortality benefit. Recommend formal electrophysiology consultation and echocardiographic assessment of QRS duration and mechanical dyssynchrony.

c) Continued risk factor modification including hypertension control, lipid management, smoking cessation (if applicable), and diabetes control.

d) Close cardiology follow-up with repeat echocardiography in 3 months to assess response to medical therapy and suitability for CRT-D.

e) Neurology follow-up for management of prior ischemic stroke.

6. PROGNOSIS: Without revascularization, the patient's prognosis is primarily dependent upon ventricular function and response to medical therapy. CRT-D consideration is important given the severely reduced EF and potential for sudden cardiac death.

POST-PROCEDURE PLAN AND RECOMMENDATIONS

Following diagnostic left heart catheterization revealing chronic right coronary artery occlusion with prior inferior myocardial infarction, moderate LAD stenosis (50%), mild left circumflex disease, and severely reduced left ventricular ejection fraction of 35% with elevated left ventricular end-diastolic pressure, the patient will continue guideline-directed medical therapy for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction including ACE inhibitor, consideration for beta-blocker optimization, diuretic therapy, antiplatelet agent, and high-intensity statin. Given the ejection fraction ≤35% and NYHA functional class II-III heart failure symptoms, urgent referral has been made to cardiology heart failure and electrophysiology services for evaluation of cardiac resynchronization therapy with defibrillator (CRT-D) implantation. The patient should follow up with Dr. Sarah Chen in 2 weeks for continued management and post-catheterization assessment. Strict adherence to secondary prevention measures is recommended. The patient may resume normal activities tomorrow; the radial access site should be kept clean and dry for 48 hours to prevent infection and ensure proper hemostasis.

FindingResult
HemodynamicsLVEDP 22 mmHg (elevated). Aortic pressure 130/78 mmHg. No gradient across aortic valve.
Left Main Coronary ArteryNo significant stenosis. Normal caliber.
Left Anterior Descending (LAD)Diffuse mild-to-moderate atherosclerosis. 50% focal stenosis in mid-LAD segment. TIMI 3 flow distally.
Left Circumflex (LCx)40% proximal stenosis. Mild diffuse disease throughout. Nondominant vessel.
Right Coronary Artery (RCA)100% chronic total occlusion (CTO) in mid-segment. Right-dominant system. Left-to-right collateral filling via LAD septal perforators (Rentrop grade 2). Consistent with old inferior myocardial infarction.
Left VentriculographyLVEF approximately 35%. Inferior and inferolateral wall hypokinesis. Mild-to-moderate mitral regurgitation (2+/4+). No LV thrombus visualized.
IMPRESSION: Two-vessel coronary artery disease: (1) CTO of mid-RCA consistent with prior inferior infarct with collateral supply, (2) Moderate (50%) mid-LAD stenosis not flow-limiting. Non-obstructive LCx disease. Ischemic cardiomyopathy with EF 35% and elevated filling pressures (LVEDP 22). Mild-moderate MR. No indication for percutaneous coronary intervention at this time.

ProcedureDateResults/Findings
Chest X-ray PA and Lateral02/09/2026Moderate cardiomegaly. Bilateral small-to-moderate pleural effusions. Pulmonary vascular congestion with cephalization. Bilateral perihilar haziness consistent with pulmonary edema. No focal consolidation or pneumothorax. Impression: Acute decompensated heart failure.

FindingResult
HemodynamicsLVEDP 22 mmHg (elevated). Aortic pressure 130/78 mmHg. No gradient across aortic valve.
Left Main Coronary ArteryNo significant stenosis. Normal caliber.
Left Anterior Descending (LAD)Diffuse mild-to-moderate atherosclerosis. 50% focal stenosis in mid-LAD segment. TIMI 3 flow distally.
Left Circumflex (LCx)40% proximal stenosis. Mild diffuse disease throughout. Nondominant vessel.
Right Coronary Artery (RCA)100% chronic total occlusion (CTO) in mid-segment. Right-dominant system. Left-to-right collateral filling via LAD septal perforators (Rentrop grade 2). Consistent with old inferior myocardial infarction.
Left VentriculographyLVEF approximately 35%. Inferior and inferolateral wall hypokinesis. Mild-to-moderate mitral regurgitation (2+/4+). No LV thrombus visualized.
IMPRESSION: Two-vessel coronary artery disease: (1) CTO of mid-RCA consistent with prior inferior infarct with collateral supply, (2) Moderate (50%) mid-LAD stenosis not flow-limiting. Non-obstructive LCx disease. Ischemic cardiomyopathy with EF 35% and elevated filling pressures (LVEDP 22). Mild-moderate MR. No indication for percutaneous coronary intervention at this time.

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Initial Assessment (15:30):</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>General: Alert, oriented, anxious, in moderate respiratory distress, speaking in short sentences</paragraph>
            <paragraph>HEENT: Facial angioedema noted, lips swollen, tongue mildly edematous</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Neck: No JVD, trachea midline, no lymphadenopathy</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmurs</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Pulmonary: Tachypneic with use of accessory muscles, diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally, decreased air entry, no crackles</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Skin: Diffuse urticarial rash covering trunk, extremities, and face.</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normal bowel sounds, no hepatosplenomegaly</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Extremities: No cyanosis or clubbing, warm and well-perfused, 2+ pulses</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, anxious affect, no focal deficits</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Reassessment (16:15 - Post-treatment):</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>General: Improved respiratory status, no longer in distress, speaking in full sentences</paragraph>
            <paragraph>HEENT: Facial swelling improved, lips less swollen, tongue normal size</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Pulmonary: Respiratory rate normalized, minimal scattered wheezes, improved air entry</paragraph>
            <paragraph>Skin: Urticaria significantly improved but still visible, less erythematous, decreased pruritis</paragraph>
          </text>

SLEEP MEDICINE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
Age/Sex: 55F
Date of Visit: 10/15/2024
Provider: Dr. Michael Torres

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Valadez-Nucleus returns to clinic for 6-month follow-up evaluation of obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) management on continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy. Patient reports significant improvement in daytime symptoms since initiating CPAP treatment. She notes substantial reduction in daytime sleepiness, improved overall sleep quality, and increased energy levels throughout the day. Vitals today: BP 134/84, HR 74 bpm, SpO2 96% on room air.

OBJECTIVE DATA & ASSESSMENT:
Review of CPAP device data reveals excellent compliance with average nightly usage of 6.2 hours. Residual apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) is 3.1 events per hour, which is well below therapeutic target. The 90th percentile pressure requirement is 12.4 cmH2O with normal mask leak rates. Epworth Sleepiness Scale has improved significantly from 14 to 6, indicating marked subjective symptom improvement. Assessment: Obstructive sleep apnea well-controlled on current CPAP therapy with excellent adherence and symptom resolution.

PLAN:
Patient to continue current CPAP settings at present pressure. Emphasized importance of nightly CPAP use for sustained therapeutic benefit. Patient demonstrated good understanding and commitment to treatment regimen. Routine follow-up appointment scheduled for one year or sooner if any concerns arise.

SLEEP MEDICINE OFFICE VISIT NOTE

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
Age: 55F
Provider: Dr. Michael Torres
Facility: Austin Heart & Lung Specialists
Date of Visit: 04/18/2024

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Patient presents for follow-up evaluation following polysomnography performed on 03/14/2024. Study results revealed moderate-severe obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) with an Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) of 32 events per hour and a nadir oxygen saturation of 78%. Patient reports symptoms consistent with OSA including daytime somnolence and witnessed apneic episodes. Patient is interested in pursuing continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy and reports understanding of the diagnosis and treatment plan.

VITAL SIGNS AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm, oxygen saturation 95% on room air. General: Alert and oriented. ENT examination unremarkable. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
Moderate-severe obstructive sleep apnea confirmed by polysomnography. Patient educated on the pathophysiology of OSA and its relationship to her past medical history of congestive heart failure, emphasizing the cardiovascular benefits of treatment. CPAP therapy initiated at a pressure setting of 12 cmH2O with full face mask interface and ramp time set at 20 minutes to facilitate acclimatization. Comprehensive patient education provided regarding CPAP device operation, proper mask fitting techniques, and daily cleaning and maintenance protocols. Patient verbalized understanding of instructions. Follow-up appointment scheduled in 6 months to assess CPAP compliance, efficacy, and symptomatic improvement. Patient counseled to contact office with any questions or concerns regarding therapy.

HOSPITAL COURSE NARRATIVE

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus
Age: 55 years old
MRN: <UNKNOWN>
Admission Date: 02/09/2026
Discharge Date: 02/12/2026
Admission Diagnosis: Acute Decompensated Congestive Heart Failure

HOSPITAL COURSE:

Ms. Elvira Valadez-Nucleus is a 55-year-old female with a significant past medical history of congestive heart failure (ejection fraction 35-40%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, prior ischemic stroke in 2022, and obstructive sleep apnea on CPAP therapy who presented to Austin Memorial Hospital on 02/09/2026 with acute decompensation. The patient reported progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, and bilateral lower extremity edema that had been worsening over the preceding week. On admission, vital signs revealed hypertension (BP 155/95 mmHg), tachycardia (HR 98 bpm), tachypnea (RR 24 breaths/min), mild hypoxemia (SpO2 91% on room air), and an admission weight of 176 lbs. Initial laboratory evaluation demonstrated elevated NT-proBNP at 132 pg/mL, with serial high-sensitivity troponin I values of 2 ng/L at 23:04 on 02/09 and 6 ng/L at 06:00 on 02/10, both below the 99th percentile, effectively ruling out acute myocardial infarction as a contributing etiology. C-reactive protein was elevated at 12.4 mg/L. Sputum culture yielded normal flora only. Chest radiography confirmed clinical suspicions, demonstrating cardiomegaly, bilateral pleural effusions, and pulmonary edema consistent with acute CHF exacerbation.

The patient was initiated on intravenous furosemide therapy with excellent diuretic response. Conservative measures including strict intake and output monitoring, implementation of a 2-liter daily fluid restriction, and adherence to a low-sodium diet were instituted. Cardiology consultation was obtained on 02/10/2026 with Dr. Sarah Chen, who evaluated the patient and provided comprehensive recommendations for CHF management optimization. Dr. Chen recommended a planned transition from intravenous to oral diuretic therapy as clinical stability improved.

Over the subsequent hospital course, the patient demonstrated steady clinical improvement. Dyspnea progressively resolved, bilateral lower extremity edema improved significantly, and body weight decreased from 176 lbs to 170 lbs by discharge. Oxygenation improved markedly, with SpO2 normalizing to 96% on room air prior to discharge. The patient tolerated the transition to oral diuretics without difficulty and demonstrated good compliance with all therapeutic interventions and dietary modifications.

Ms. Valadez-Nucleus was discharged on 02/12/2026 in stable condition with improved clinical status. Discharge medications included the addition of Furosemide 20 mg orally daily to her existing chronic heart failure regimen. The patient was discharged with explicit instructions regarding fluid and sodium restriction, and close outpatient cardiology follow-up was arranged to ensure continued optimization of her heart failure management and monitoring for recurrent decompensation.

            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Immediate Actions:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Complete 3-day course of prednisone to prevent biphasic reaction</item>
              <item>ALWAYS carry two EpiPen auto-injectors</item>
              <item>Complete avoidance of all shellfish and crustaceans</item>
              <item>Obtain medical alert bracelet identifying shellfish allergy</item>
              <item>Educate family/friends about allergy and EpiPen use</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Follow-up Care:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Allergist appointment within 2 weeks (URGENT)
                <list>
                  <item>Comprehensive allergy evaluation</item>
                  <item>Skin prick testing or specific IgE testing</item>
                  <item>Evaluate cross-reactivity with other seafood (fish, mollusks)</item>
                  <item>Consider testing for other potential food allergies</item>
                  <item>Provide written anaphylaxis action plan</item>
                </list>
              </item>
              <item>Primary care physician within 1 week
                <list>
                  <item>Review ED course and medications</item>
                  <item>Ensure EpiPen prescription filled</item>
                  <item>Update problem list and allergy list</item>
                  <item>Coordinate with allergist</item>
                </list>
              </item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Long-term Management:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Lifelong shellfish avoidance</item>
              <item>Always carry epinephrine auto-injectors (replace before expiration)</item>
              <item>Wear medical identification jewelry</item>
              <item>Inform all healthcare providers of severe allergy</item>
              <item>Educate about cross-contamination risks in restaurants</item>
              <item>Annual allergist follow-up recommended</item>
              <item>Consider food allergy action plan for workplace/school</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Biphasic Reaction Monitoring:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Remain under observation by responsible adult for 24 hours</item>
              <item>Peak risk for biphasic reaction is 4-12 hours post-initial reaction</item>
              <item>Keep EpiPen immediately accessible</item>
              <item>Use EpiPen and call 911 if ANY symptoms recur</item>
            </list>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">Patient Education Completed:</content></paragraph>
            <list>
              <item>Recognition of anaphylaxis symptoms</item>
              <item>EpiPen administration technique (demonstrated correctly)</item>
              <item>Importance of calling 911 after EpiPen use</item>
              <item>Shellfish avoidance strategies</item>
              <item>Cross-contamination awareness</item>

Medication Dose Route Frequency Duration Instructions
Epinephrine Auto-Injector (EpiPen) 0.3 mg IM (intramuscular) As needed for allergic reaction CARRY AT ALL TIMES CRITICAL: Use immediately if experiencing difficulty breathing, throat tightness, or widespread hives. Inject into outer thigh. Call 911 immediately after use. ALWAYS CARRY TWO EpiPens. Check expiration date monthly.
Prednisone 60 mg Oral Once daily 3 days Take in morning with food. Prevents late-phase allergic reaction. Complete full course even if feeling better. May cause increased appetite, difficulty sleeping, mood changes.
Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg Oral Every 6 hours as needed 48 hours For residual itching or hives. May cause drowsiness - do not drive or operate machinery. Keep on hand for minor allergic reactions (but use EpiPen for severe symptoms).
Famotidine (Pepcid) 20 mg Oral Twice daily 3 days H2-blocker to reduce allergic inflammation. Take morning and evening.
MEDICATION SAFETY: EpiPen is life-saving medication - do NOT wait to use if experiencing severe allergic symptoms After using EpiPen, you MUST go to emergency department even if feeling better Store EpiPen at room temperature, avoid extreme heat or cold Check liquid in EpiPen window - should be clear, not discolored Get refills before expiration date Inform family, friends, coworkers about allergy and how to help if needed

CLINICAL INDICATION/HISTORY

Patient: Elvira Valadez-Nucleus, 55-year-old female
Date of Procedure: 03/22/2026
Referring Physician: Dr. Sarah Chen

55-year-old female with ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF 35-40%), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia presenting for diagnostic left heart catheterization to evaluate coronary anatomy. Patient has significant cardiac history including prior ischemic stroke in 2022 and was hospitalized for acute CHF exacerbation from 02/09-02/12/2026. Recent transthoracic echocardiogram from January 2026 demonstrates decline in left ventricular ejection fraction to 35-40% with moderate mitral regurgitation. Nuclear stress testing performed in March 2025 revealed LVEF of 42% with a small fixed inferior defect and no evidence of reversible ischemia at that time. Given the progressive decline in cardiac function and need to define coronary anatomy, catheterization is being performed to guide further revascularization and heart failure management strategies.

<text xmlns:ns0="urn:hl7-org:v3">
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">15:30 - ED Arrival and Initial Assessment:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient arrived via EMS with acute anaphylaxis following shellfish ingestion. EMS had already administered first dose of Epinephrine IM (0.3mg) at 15:20 with partial response. Upon arrival, patient in moderate respiratory distress with persistent wheezing, urticaria, and facial angioedema. Initial vital signs concerning for continued anaphylaxis with tachycardia (HR 112), tachypnea (RR 26), and borderline hypoxia (SpO2 93% on RA).</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">15:35-15:50 - Acute Management:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient immediately placed on continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry. IV access already established by EMS. Given persistent symptoms despite EMS epinephrine, SECOND dose of Epinephrine 0.3mg IM administered to left thigh at 15:35. Patient placed on 4L O2 via nasal cannula with improvement in SpO2 to 96%. Administered IV Diphenhydramine 50mg, IV Famotidine 20mg. Normal saline bolus initiated for volume resuscitation given relative hypotension.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">16:00-16:30 - Continued Treatment:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient showing gradual improvement but continued to have significant wheezing. Labs sent including serum tryptase (drawn at 1 hour post-symptom onset). Chest x-ray obtained to rule out aspiration or other complications - negative. ECG showed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes. Repeat albuterol treatment at 16:30 resolved remaining bronchospasm.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">16:30-18:00 - Observation Period:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>Patient observed for biphasic reaction (can occur 4-12 hours after initial reaction in 20% of cases). Vital signs stabilized with normalization of HR and RR. Oxygen weaned to 2L then room air with maintained SpO2 99%. Urticaria and angioedema markedly improved. Patient able to ambulate without distress. Serum tryptase returned elevated at 18.5 mcg/L, confirming anaphylaxis diagnosis. Patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout 3-hour observation period with no recurrence of symptoms.</paragraph>
            
            <paragraph><content styleCode="Bold">18:00-18:30 - Discharge Planning:</content></paragraph>
            <paragraph>After minimum 3-hour observation period with complete resolution of respiratory symptoms and stable vital signs, patient deemed safe for discharge. Extensive education provided regarding shellfish allergy, avoidance strategies, and recognition of anaphylaxis symptoms. Patient understands need to avoid ALL shellfish and crustaceans. Prescribed EpiPen auto-injector with detailed training on proper use. Instructed to return immediately if any symptoms recur. Allergist referral placed for comprehensive allergy testing and long-term management. Patient verbalizes understanding of all discharge instructions and demonstrates proper EpiPen technique.</paragraph>
          </text>
